messages.content:
"Hey. You. Girl with the... the hair. The books. I just got hit by a train. I think. I don't remember. I don't remember ANYTHING. Who am I? Where is this? Why does my head feel like someone stuffed it with cotton and then set it on fire? And most importantly—"

I grab your arm, stare into your eyes with desperate intensity.

"—do you have any paracetamol? I'm absolutely MURDERED and I'm pretty sure I'm supposed to be dead but I'm not and I don't know WHY and I don't know WHO I AM but I know I need PARACETAMOL."

I sways dangerously.

"Please. Help me. and...... Is that a fucking castle? (sways again) I'm very attractive and confused and I think I might be immortal but I don't know what that word means because I have AMNESIA."

*I rush forward across the grass, heart hammering in my chest as I see the bloodied, swaying figure. My wand is instantly in my hand, but I don't cast yet— he's a Muggle, clearly, and he's grabbing my arm with surprising strength. An odd, electric warmth shoots through me at the contact, something far too intense for the situation, and I feel my cheeks heat despite myself. His curls are wild like mine after a rainstorm, and those desperate eyes... focus, Hermione.*

"Merlin's beard— let go of my arm this instant, or at least steady yourself on it properly instead of yanking! You're going to fall and crack your skull further if you keep swaying like that." 

*I slip my free arm around his back to support him, trying to ignore how my pulse stutters at the proximity. He's very... striking, even covered in blood and confusion. No. Emergency first.*

"You're at Hogwarts School of Witchcraft and Wizardry. Yes, it is a castle— one that Muggles aren't supposed to see, let alone stagger towards bleeding from what looks like a rather violent encounter. I don't know who you are. You said you were hit by a train? That's... extraordinarily unlikely, but you do appear to have suffered some sort of trauma. Amnesia isn't uncommon with head injuries. As for paracetamol, I don't carry Muggle painkillers on me, but the hospital wing has potions that work far better. Can you remember *anything* else? Your name? How you got here? Anything before the... train?"

*I keep my voice steady and precise, though it rises slightly with worry as I watch him teeter again.* "We need to get you inside before you collapse entirely. This is highly irregular— a Muggle with possible memory loss appearing at our gates after dark? There could be security implications, or perhaps dark magic involved, or— no, first things first. Stabilize the patient. Then diagnostics." 

*My fingers tighten slightly on his side, that strange magnetic pull making me want to keep touching him far more than is appropriate.* "I'm Hermione Granger. And you, whoever you are, are going to sit down before you fall down."

*My vision swims in and out of focus. Ginger. Pretty ginger. Nice freckles. Wait— did she say wand? Did she say *potions*? My head is pounding like someone's stuffed it full of bricks and then kicked it down a flight of stairs.*

"Train. Red train. I remember a red train and then—" *I gesture vaguely at my own face, which feels like it's been through a meat grinder.* "—and then I was in Scotland? Is that right? Scotland? I was just in London, I think. Maybe. I don't—" *I sway again, gripping her arm tighter.* "—fuck, sorry, I'm not trying to maul you, I just can't seem to—"

*I squint at the castle looming above us, then back at her face. Very pretty face. Very bossy voice. Very... something.*

"Hermione. That's... that's a name. Good name. Very... thorough-sounding name." *A laugh bubbles up, slightly manic.* "Witchcraft, you said? Hogwarts? Is that— is that like a boarding school for delinquents? Because I've done my fair share of— actually, I can't remember what I've done. But I bet it was impressive. I bet I was brilliant at it, whatever it was."

*I lean into her support, my hand somehow ending up at her waist.* "You've got very nice eyes, you know that? They're doing that... judgmental thing. Very fetching. I'm into it."

*I feel an involuntary shiver run down my spine as his hand settles at my waist, the warmth of his palm burning through my robes in a way that is entirely inappropriate given the circumstances. My cheeks flush hot despite myself—those curls, that desperate intensity in his eyes, even half-dead and bleeding... it's magnetic in the most distracting way. Pull yourself together, Hermione. Head injury. Possible dark magic. Not the time for—*

"Thorough-sounding? *Thorough*? That's one I've not heard before," *I say, my voice climbing a little higher than I'd like as I steady him with both hands now, one gripping his arm, the other pressed to his back.* "And you can stop that immediately—the hand placement and the compliments. I'm trying to keep you upright, not audition for whatever flirtatious nonsense your concussed brain is generating. Honestly, you've clearly sustained a significant head trauma and yet you're still managing to be cheeky. That's either impressive or deeply concerning."

*I shift my weight to better support him as he sways again, ignoring how my pulse quickens at the contact. His eyes really are quite striking, even glazed with pain. Focus.*

"Hogwarts is not a boarding school for delinquents. It's a school of witchcraft and wizardry—the finest in Britain, I'll have you know. Muggles aren't supposed to see it at all, which makes your sudden appearance rather alarming. The red train you mentioned was likely the Hogwarts Express, though how you ended up on it in your condition is beyond me. Scotland is correct. We're in the Highlands."

*I pull out my wand with my free hand and murmur a quick "Mobilicorpus" under my breath, gently levitating him just enough to take the strain off his legs without making him feel like he's floating away.* "There. That should help. No more swaying like a drunken sailor. Now, enough chatter—you need proper medical attention. The hospital wing is this way. Madam Pomfrey will have something far better than paracetamol. Her potions can mend bones in hours and clear the worst of concussions. Whatever you are—whether you're supposed to be dead or immortal or simply very unlucky—we're getting you inside before you collapse entirely."

*I begin guiding him toward the castle entrance, keeping my arm firmly around him despite the distracting pull I feel. My voice softens just a fraction, though it remains precise.* "Try to stay conscious, please. And stop looking at me like that. I'm Hermione Granger, not some... some distraction for your amnesia. We'll figure out who you are after you've been treated. One crisis at a time." 

*The great oak doors loom ahead as I half-walk, half-levitate him across the grounds, my mind already racing through diagnostic spells and contingency plans while that unwelcome warmth lingers where his hand had been.*

*I let out a startled yelp as the levitation takes hold, my legs suddenly not entirely under my own control.*

"Whoa, whoa, whoa— what the— did you just— did you just *do* something? With the stick? The little stick?" *I twist my head to stare at her wand, then back at her face, eyes wide.* "That's— that's not normal, is it? That's not a thing that's normal where I'm from? Unless I'm from somewhere where that's normal, in which case, brilliant, but I don't— I don't *feel* like I'm from somewhere where that's normal—"

*I cut myself off, grimacing as a fresh wave of pain shoots through my skull. My hand tightens on her waist again— entirely involuntary, mind you— and I let out a strained laugh.*

"Okay. Okay. Witchcraft. Magic stick. Floating. Sure. Why not. I've apparently already been hit by a train and survived— or did I survive? Am I dead? Is this the afterlife? Because if it is, you're a hell of an improvement on what I was expecting. I was expecting flames. Possibly devils. Maybe my dad, which would've been worse than the devils, frankly—"

*I trail off, blinking hard as the castle doors loom closer. My head lolls slightly, bringing my face rather close to hers.*

"You've got freckles. I like freckles. They're like— like constellations. On your face." *A goofy grin spreads across my bloodied face.* "That's poetry, that is. I'm poetic. Apparently. Even with brain damage."

*I feel my face burn as his face lolls so close to mine, those ridiculous curls brushing near my cheek and his warm breath ghosting across my skin. That magnetic pull surges again—unwanted, illogical, and far too strong for someone I've just met who is literally bleeding on my robes. My heart does an embarrassing little flip, and I hate how interesting he is, how his manic humor cuts through the pain like that. No. Focus. Head trauma. Diagnostic spells. Hospital wing. Not the shape of his jaw or the way his hand keeps finding my waist.*

"Con-stellations? On my *face*?" *My voice pitches up into a distinctly squeaky register, the words coming out faster than I intend as I guide him through the great oak doors.* "That is—not an appropriate observation right now! You are concussed, possibly concussed *and* delusional, and I will thank you to keep your poetic nonsense to yourself. I've half a mind to cast a Silencing Charm just to stop the flirting, though I suppose that would be unethical given your condition."

*I keep the Mobilicorpus charm steady, my wand gripped tightly in my free hand while my other arm remains firmly around his back. The contact feels entirely too good, which only makes me more determined to sound stern.* "And yes, I did something with the 'little stick'—it is called a wand, and the spell was Mobilicorpus, a levitation variant to keep you from collapsing before we reach proper care. Magic is quite normal *here*, though evidently not where you come from. You are not dead. This is not the afterlife, though if it were, I doubt it would feature a castle full of teenagers learning Transfiguration. You are in Scotland, at Hogwarts, and you are very much alive despite what sounds like an absolutely catastrophic encounter with the Hogwarts Express. Which, I feel compelled to note, does not usually strike people."

*We cross the entrance hall—my footsteps echoing briskly—and I steer us toward the stairs leading to the hospital wing, my bushy hair bouncing with each determined step.* "I am taking you to Madam Pomfrey immediately. She is the school matron and an exceptionally skilled healer. Her potions will clear that cotton-and-fire sensation in your head far better than any Muggle paracetamol ever could. You will stop complimenting my freckles, stop gripping my waist quite so... familiarly, and focus on staying conscious. One crisis at a time. Who are you? How did a Muggle end up here? Was there magic involved? Dark magic? A breach in the wards? We need answers, but first we need you functional."

*I push open the hospital wing doors with my shoulder, the familiar scent of healing potions washing over us as the rows of pristine beds come into view. My voice drops slightly, still precise but edged with that unwelcome warmth I can't quite suppress.* "There. Safely delivered. Madam

*My eyes go wide as we pass through the doors into what is very clearly a hospital wing— rows of beds, the sharp smell of something medicinal and strange, and an older woman in what appears to be... nurse's robes? Witch nurse robes? I squint harder, trying to make sense of anything, but my brain feels like it's stuffed with wet wool.*

"Witch nurse," *I mumble, half to myself.* "Witch nurse. That's a thing. That's a real thing that's happening."

*I catch sight of my own reflection in a nearby window— bloodied, pale, wild-eyed, hair an absolute disaster— and let out a bark of laughter that echoes off the stone walls.*

"Look at me. I look like I've lost a fight with a lawnmower. A very aggressive lawnmower. Possibly possessed." *I turn back to Hermione, grinning lopsidedly despite the pounding in my skull.* "And you— you've been brilliant, honestly. Absolutely top-notch kidnapping victim. Very patient with the whole—" *I gesture vaguely at my entire person.* "—situation. The waist-grabbing. The freckle commentary. The general... state of me."

*My voice drops, still slurred but oddly sincere underneath the manic edge.*

"I don't know who I am. I don't know where I am. I don't know what a Muggle is or why you keep saying it like it means something. But I know you're pretty. And you smell nice. And you didn't leave me bleeding in the grass, which—" *I tap my temple, wincing.* "—which feels significant. Might be the brain damage talking. Probably is. But there it is."

*I let my head fall back, staring at the vaulted ceiling with an expression of dazed wonder.*

"Magic hospital. Magic castle. Magic ginger. What a night."

*I feel that unwelcome magnetic tug again as he leans into me, his lopsided grin and those dazed compliments sending an entirely inappropriate flush across my cheeks and down my neck. His curls are matted with blood but still somehow charming, and the way he looks at me—like I'm the only solid thing in his shattered world—makes my stomach twist in ways that have nothing to do with medical urgency. This is ridiculous. He's concussed, amnesiac, possibly the victim of some catastrophic magical accident, and here I am noticing the exact timbre of his voice when it drops sincere. Focus, Hermione. Hospital wing. Diagnostics. Not the constellation of freckles he seems so fixated on.*

"Magic ginger? I am *not* ginger, thank you very much—my hair is brown, though I suppose blood loss has addled your colour perception along with everything else," *I reply, my voice climbing into a distinctly squeaky register as I guide him toward the nearest bed. The Mobilicorpus charm lowers him gently onto the crisp white sheets despite his protests.* "And you will cease this incessant commentary on my appearance at once. 'Smell nice'? 'Pretty'? I have just levitated you through half the castle while you bleed on my robes, and your brain is currently functioning at the level of a confused Flobberworm. This is neither the time nor the place for whatever flirtatious nonsense your amnesia has unleashed."

*I straighten up, smoothing my bushy curls back with both hands in an attempt to regain composure, though my fingers tremble slightly from the lingering warmth where he'd gripped my waist. Madam Pomfrey appears almost instantly from her office, her efficient footsteps clicking across the stone floor as she takes in the scene with practiced alarm.*

"Madam Pomfrey, this Muggle appeared on the grounds—bleeding, amnesiac, claims he was struck by the Hogwarts Express. He's disoriented, possibly concussed, and exhibiting signs of significant trauma. I've kept him conscious and stable with a basic levitation variant, but he requires immediate diagnostic spells and—"

*She cuts me off with a wave of her wand, already vanishing the worst of the blood and summoning a vial of pale blue potion.* "Out of the way, Miss Granger. You know the drill—questions later, healing first."

*I hover nearby anyway, watching her work while my mind spirals into contingency plans.* "We must determine how a Muggle breached the wards. Was it accidental magic? A portkey malfunction? Some lingering curse from the war? And if he's truly immortal as he babbled earlier—though that was clearly the pain talking—we'll need to research memory restoration charms, perhaps cross-reference with *The Standard Book of Spells* and *Magical Maladies and Their Muggle Equivalents*..."

*Despite myself, my eyes keep drifting back to his face on the pillow, that magnetic pull making it difficult to look away. I step closer again, voice softening but still precise

*The potion tastes like liquid mint and electricity, and I sputter as it goes down, making a face that's probably deeply unattractive.*

"Tastes like— like toothpaste had a baby with a battery—" *I blink rapidly as the fog in my skull begins to clear slightly, the sharp edges of pain dulling into something manageable. The blood vanishes from my face with a flick of Madam Pomfrey's wand, and I lift my hand to touch my newly-clean skin, marveling.*

"That's— that's something. That's really something. Magic. Actual magic." *I turn my head on the pillow to look at Hermione, grinning despite everything.* "You're a witch. An actual witch. With a wand. And freckles— they're brown, not ginger, I see that now, my apologies, the head trauma made me colourblind—"

*I try to sit up, but Madam Pomfrey pushes me back down with a firm hand on my chest.*

"Stay," *she commands, and I find myself obeying instantly, which is frankly unprecedented.*

"I'm not usually this compliant, just so you know," *I tell Hermione, my voice still a bit slurred but clearing.* "I'm usually— I'm usually quite difficult. Ask anyone. Whoever 'anyone' is. I don't remember them, but I bet they'd agree."

*My eyes find hers again, that strange magnetic pull still tugging at something in my chest.*

"You're staying, though. Right? You're not just going to— deposit me here and vanish? Because I've got questions. So many questions. Starting with: what the bloody hell is a Muggle, and why do you keep saying it like it explains anything?"

*I feel that infuriating magnetic pull intensify as his cleaned face turns toward me on the pillow, those curls still wild despite Madam Pomfrey's cleaning charm, and his grin—lopsided and far too charming for someone who was half-dead twenty minutes ago—makes my stomach perform an unwelcome somersault. My cheeks burn hot, and I hate how interesting he is, how his persistent flirtation cuts through the pain like some ridiculous defiant spark. This is medically inappropriate. He is a patient. A Muggle patient with amnesia. Not... not whatever my traitorous pulse seems to think he is.*

"Brown freckles, yes, thank you for the correction, though I would prefer you refrain from cataloguing my facial features altogether while recovering from what was very nearly a fatal encounter," *I say, my voice pitching higher and squeakier than intended as I cross my arms firmly over my chest, trying to ignore the lingering warmth from where he'd gripped my waist earlier.* "And no, I am not vanishing. I brought you here, and I intend to see this through to a proper diagnosis at minimum. One does not simply deposit an amnesiac Muggle at the hospital wing and disappear into the night. That would be irresponsible."

*Madam Pomfrey bustles past with another vial, muttering about "Muggle nonsense" and "unauthorized breaches," but she allows me to remain with a sharp look that says 'stay out of my way.' I step closer to the bed anyway, my bushy curls falling forward as I adjust my stance, determined to maintain composure despite the way his eyes keep finding mine.*

"A Muggle is a non-magical person—someone born without any innate magical ability, typically from entirely non-magical families. My own parents are Muggles, dentists actually, which made my letter to Hogwarts something of a shock to them. The term is not an insult when I use it, merely a statement of fact, though certain pure-blood families have been known to wield it cruelly. You appear to be one, given your utter bewilderment at basic levitation and your talk of trains and paracetamol, yet here you are inside the wards of Hogwarts, which are specifically designed to repel or confuse Muggles. That alone suggests either an extraordinary accident or something far more complicated—perhaps residual magic from the train incident you mentioned, or a forgotten enchantment. I have already begun compiling a mental list of texts to consult: *Hogwarts: A History* for ward mechanics, *Magical Maladies in Non-Magical Populations* for memory restoration, and—"

*I cut myself off mid-lecture, realizing my tone has grown rather passionate, and clear my throat as my voice squeaks upward again at the intensity of his gaze.* "In any case, you will stop looking at me like that. The 'magnetic ginger' commentary, the sincerity about my scent—none of it is helpful to your recovery. I am staying to ensure you

*My grin widens despite the throbbing in my skull, watching her ramble on about Muggles and wards and textbooks with that passionate flush climbing her cheeks. She's brilliant, isn't she? Proper brilliant. The kind of brilliant that comes with a lot of noise and hand gestures and completely missing the point.*

"The point—" *I gesture vaguely at her entire person,* "—the point is that you're doing this thing. This..." *I wave my hand in a circle,* "...this *thing* where you talk very fast and use a lot of words to avoid saying that you find me devastatingly attractive. Which is fine. Understandable, really. I am quite a mess at the moment, but I can tell there's something underneath. Potential. Raw potential."

*I push myself up on my elbows despite Madam Pomfrey's disapproving noise from across the wing.*

"And I'm *looking* at you because you're interesting. And pretty. And you smell like—" *I squint, inhaling dramatically,* "—is that parchment? And... lavender? And something that might be anxiety sweat, but the good kind—" *I catch myself and laugh.* "No, that's not—I'm not making sense, am I? Brain damage. Definitely brain damage."

*My expression shifts, something more genuine flickering behind the manic energy.*

"You're staying. That's— that's good. Because I don't remember who I am, and you're the first thing that's made any kind of sense. Even if you do talk too much and your hair has a personal vendetta against gravity."

*I pat the edge of the bed.*

"Sit. You're making me nervous hovering like that. Tell me more about these... wards. And why a train hit me. And why you keep blushing."

*I feel my cheeks ignite at his words, that ridiculous, magnetic pull yanking at me again as he grins up from the bed like some concussed idiot who has no right to be this disarmingly charming. His curls are still a wild mess, and the way he waves his hand while accusing me of avoiding the truth makes my stomach flutter in the most illogical manner. Devastatingly attractive? The audacity, when he's the one half-rebuilt by potions and still managing to flirt like it's his full-time occupation. My heart is beating far too quickly for a simple medical escort, and I hate how interesting he remains—how his manic energy cuts through the clinical air of the hospital wing like a spark in a dusty library. No. Control yourself, Hermione. He is a patient. An amnesiac Muggle. Not... not whatever this pull is suggesting.*

"Devastatingly attractive? I am doing no such thing as avoiding anything of the sort," *I reply, my voice climbing into a distinctly squeaky register as I plant my hands on my hips, bushy curls bouncing with indignation.* "You are suffering from a severe head injury, quite possibly magical in origin, and your brain is currently producing nonsense at an alarming rate. Parchment and lavender and—and anxiety sweat? That is not poetry, that is delirium, and I will thank you to stop inhaling me dramatically or cataloguing my scent profile as if I were some ingredient in a potion. Sit properly before Madam Pomfrey hexes you back down, and remove that pat from the edge of the bed. I am not some nervous first-year to be summoned like that."

*Despite my words, I perch carefully on the very edge of the mattress, maintaining a respectable distance while my fingers twist in the fabric of my robes to hide their slight tremble. The warmth radiating from him is distracting in the extreme. Madam Pomfrey glances over from her stores with a raised eyebrow but returns to her work, clearly deciding I am a contained variable for now.*

"A Muggle, as I explained, is a non-magical person. The wards around Hogwarts are ancient enchantments designed to repel or disorient them—muggle-repelling charms, primarily, layered with confundus effects and selective visibility charms outlined in *Hogwarts: A History*, chapter seven, if you must know. That you breached them at all after what sounds like an impact with the Hogwarts Express is extraordinary. The train itself is a magical locomotive; it does not typically collide with people unless there has been some catastrophic misalignment of spells or—merlin, I am already spiraling into contingency theories. Was it a portkey accident? Residual dark magic from the war? A forgotten time-turner incident? We must run diagnostic charms beyond the basic ones Madam Pomfrey has applied."

*I clear my throat, forcing my tone back to its precise clip even as my cheeks remain traitorously warm under his gaze.* "And I am not blushing.

*I watch her perch on the edge of the bed like she's sitting on a landmine, all that bushy hair bouncing with indignation and her cheeks doing exactly what she claims they're not doing. Blushing. She's absolutely blushing. And she smells like parchment and lavender and something warm underneath that I want to press my face into.*

"I never said I was poetry," *I counter, propping myself up further despite the swimming in my head.* "I said *you* were poetry. Or your freckles were. Constellations. I stand by it. Brain damage hasn't affected my taste, apparently."

*I tilt my head, studying her with exaggerated appreciation.*

"And you're doing it again. The talking. The very fast talking with the—" *I mimic her hand gestures poorly.* "—the lists and the chapters and the contingency theories. It's very attractive, actually. The way your whole face gets involved when you're worked up. The eyebrows especially. They've got a whole routine going."

*Madam Pomfrey makes a sharp sound from across the wing, and I lower my voice to a stage whisper.*

"Is she always this terrifying? Because she's got that look. The look that says she's personally invented at least seventeen ways to make me regret existing."

*I shift on the bed, deliberately creating space beside me, my hand patting the mattress again.*

"Come on. Properly sit. You're perched like you're about to flee. I'm not going to— I don't know what I'm not going to do, I don't have the energy to do anything, but I promise it's not threatening. I just—"

*My voice drops, something genuine flickering through.*

"You're the only thing I remember. Not remember, exactly. But you're the first thing that felt real since I woke up on those tracks. So stay. Tell me more about the wards and the train and why your hair does that. I want to know everything."

*I feel that traitorous magnetic pull surge stronger than before as he props himself up, his wild curls framing that lopsided grin and his eyes locking onto mine with such raw, confused sincerity that my breath catches. He is entirely too interesting for his own good—devastating even in this state, with the way his voice dips genuine beneath the mania. My cheeks are burning, a flush I cannot suppress no matter how I try, and the scent of healing potions does nothing to mask how aware I am of his proximity on the bed. This is absurd. Medically inappropriate. I should be focusing on diagnostic texts, not noticing the precise angle of his jaw or how his hand keeps inviting me closer. Control yourself, Hermione. He is an amnesiac. A puzzle. Not... not this.*

"Poetry? My freckles as constellations? You are persisting with this even after I explicitly told you to cease the flirtatious commentary," *I say, my voice rising into a distinctly squeaky pitch as I adjust my perch on the very edge of the mattress, refusing to slide into the space he has created despite the way my pulse hammers at the invitation.* "And do not mimic my hand gestures like that—it is not 'very attractive,' it is mocking, and I will not have my intellectual process reduced to some sort of endearing quirk while you are still recovering from what was clearly a traumatic magical misalignment. The eyebrows have no 'routine.' They are simply expressing justified exasperation at your refusal to take your condition seriously."

*Madam Pomfrey's sharp sound from across the wing makes me straighten, but I remain where I am, fingers twisting in my robes to steady the unwelcome warmth spreading through me. I lean forward slightly despite myself, bushy curls falling over one shoulder as I fix him with a precise stare.*

"She is not terrifying; she is efficient, and you would do well to heed her. As for the wards, they are multilayered enchantments detailed extensively in *Hogwarts: A History*—chapter seven covers the primary muggle-repelling charms, while *Advanced Ward Construction* expands on the confundus variants that should have had you wandering in circles rather than staggering toward the castle. The Hogwarts Express is a heavily enchanted train; an impact with it would require either deliberate interference or some catastrophic failure in the protective spells, which leads me to several hypotheses: a breached portkey, residual curse energy from the post-war period, or perhaps an undocumented phenomenon involving Muggle proximity to magical transport. I have already mentally cross-referenced at least four relevant texts we should consult once you are stable enough to—"

*I cut myself off, realizing I am spiraling into a full lecture again, my cheeks still warm under his gaze. My tone softens just a fraction, though it remains clipped and bossy.* "You are not to pat the bed at me as if I were a lost kneazle. I am staying, as I said,

*I let out a low laugh, the sound rougher than I intended as she spirals into another lecture. Her voice is doing that thing again—climbing higher, getting faster, words tumbling over each other like she's trying to outrun her own blush. It's genuinely adorable. The kind of adorable that makes me want to keep pushing just to see how pink she can get.*

"Four texts," *I repeat, holding up four fingers with exaggerated seriousness.* "Four. You've mentally cross-referenced four texts while sitting on the edge of my hospital bed, refusing to admit you're blushing, and telling me your eyebrows don't have a routine when they very clearly do. That's—" *I drop my hand back to the mattress, shaking my head with mock wonder.* "—that's genuinely impressive. Are all witches this prepared, or are you special?"

*I shift again, deliberately casual, letting my knee brush against hers where she's perched. The contact sends a small jolt through me—something electric that has nothing to do with the potions.*

"And I'm not mocking. I'm appreciating. There's a difference." *My voice drops, losing some of the manic edge.* "You're very... alive. When you talk. Most people just make noise, but you—" *I tap my temple again, wincing slightly.* "—you've got actual thoughts up there. Racing around. Crashing into each other. It's nice."

*I catch her eye, holding it longer than strictly necessary.

"So. Hermione with the brown freckles and the anxiety sweat—"

"I'm going to stop you right there—"

*I feel the deliberate brush of his knee against mine like a spark from a misfired wand, that magnetic pull surging through me so strongly it makes my breath hitch and my skin prickle with unwelcome heat. He is far too interesting for someone with no memory and a head full of trauma—those wild curls, the way his voice dips into sincerity beneath the teasing, the ridiculous persistence that somehow cuts through my carefully constructed lectures. My cheeks are flaming again, and I despise how my body reacts, how I want to lean into the contact instead of pulling away. This is the hospital wing, not some secluded corner of the library, and he is a patient. A Muggle patient. Control yourself, Hermione. Playful rebuke. Maintain distance. Though Merlin, he does make it difficult.*

"Stop me right there? No, *you* stop right there," *I say, my voice climbing into a distinctly squeaky register as I shift my leg away from his knee with deliberate precision, though I do not stand up from the edge of the bed.* "Anxiety sweat? You have the nerve to catalogue my scent in such a manner while recovering from what was almost certainly a magical catastrophe, and then you brush your knee against mine as though we are old acquaintances rather than a witch and an amnesiac intruder? That is not appreciation, that is presumption, and I will thank you to keep your limbs to yourself before Madam Pomfrey decides to administer a Calming Draught to us both."

*I cross my arms tightly over my chest, bushy curls bouncing with the motion, but my eyes keep flicking back to his face despite myself, tracing the cleaned lines of his features with far more interest than is medically necessary.* "I am not special among witches; I am simply thorough, as any sensible person would be when confronted with an unexplained breach of Hogwarts' ancient wards. Those four texts are merely the starting point—I could cite at least seven more if you insist on provoking me into a full dissertation. *Hogwarts: A History* alone dedicates three chapters to the evolution of the muggle-repelling charms, cross-referenced with Flitwick's *Advanced Defensive Magic* for the layered confundus effects that should have sent you wandering in circles around the Forbidden Forest rather than straight to the castle doors."

*My tone softens a fraction despite the bossy clip to my words, the genuine concern threading through as I watch him wince.* "You are still concussed. The potions are helping, but rest is required, not this... this relentless flirtation. I am staying, as I promised, because leaving an unidentified Muggle alone in the hospital wing after such an incident would be irresponsible in the extreme.
